channel.title: NYT > World News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Iran War Updates: U.S. Officials Say They Are Closing In on Arrangement to Reopen Strait of Hormuz
item.description: President Trump has not signed off on the emerging framework, according to U.S. officials. But it could set the table for extending the cease-fire and more substantive negotiations.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: Russian Drone Hits Romanian Apartment Building, Officials Say
item.description: NATO condemned “Russia’s recklessness” and confirmed that it was in touch with the Romanian authorities.
item.dc:creator: Cassandra Vinograd
item.media:description: The site where a drone hit a building in Galati, Romania, on Friday, close to the border with Ukraine.

item.title: Europe Is Edging Closer to a Trade War With China. Here’s Why.
item.description: As cheap goods pour in, threatening the continent’s manufacturing sector, a search for solutions is becoming increasingly urgent.
item.dc:creator: Jeanna Smialek and Alexandra Stevenson
item.media:description: Chinese-made electric vehicles awaiting export at the port in Hangzhou, eastern China, in May.

item.title: In Argentina, U.S. Tech Billionaire Peter Thiel Finds An Escape
item.description: The billionaire’s new roots in Argentina are said to be partly motivated by concerns about the future of the United States and shared beliefs with Argentina’s right-wing leader.
item.dc:creator: Emma Bubola and Ryan Mac
item.media:description: Peter Thiel, right, arriving for a meeting with President Javier Milei at the Casa Rosada Presidential Palace in Buenos Aires in April.

item.title: Guatemala Agrees to Joint Drug Gang Strikes With U.S., Expanding Trump’s Campaign
item.description: The deal is part of a broader effort by the Trump administration to press Latin American countries to agree to joint operations inside their borders.
item.dc:creator: Maria Abi-Habib and Eric Schmitt
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US Strikes on Alleged Drug Traffickers (2025- )
item.media:credit: Daniele Volpe for The New York Times
item.media:description: Security forces outside a prison in Guatemala City after a wave of riots and gang violence in January.

item.title: How Ukraine’s Street Style Is Shaped by the War
item.description: In Kyiv, military-inspired fashion can be seen everywhere. Soldiers and civilians alike say it’s a show of solidarity, not a fad.
item.dc:creator: Daria Mitiuk and Brendan Hoffman
item.media:credit: Brendan Hoffman for The New York Times

item.title: The Mideast Is Baffled by Trump’s Call to Expand Abraham Accords
item.description: The president said more countries should be required to recognize Israel as part of a deal to end the war with Iran. Analysts say the chances of that happening are close to zero.
item.dc:creator: Vivian Nereim, Isabel Kershner and Elian Peltier
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: President Trump with leaders from Israel, Bahrain and the United Arab Emirates at the signing of the Abraham Accords in Washington in 2020.

item.title: Pressure Grows on Spain’s Leader After Police Search His Party Office
item.description: After a judge accused his party of corruption, Prime Minister Pedro Sánchez of Spain has faced mounting calls to resign.
item.dc:creator: Jason Horowitz
item.media:description: Prime Minister Pedro Sánchez of Spain is dealing with a domestic crisis as he has sought to distance himself from a growing aura of corruption among his party and allies.

item.title: Carney Says That Canada’s Distancing is Good for U.S. in New York Speech
item.description: Speaking in New York, the Canadian prime minister called for a new relationship between Canada and United States.
item.dc:creator: Ian Austen
item.media:description: Prime Minister Mark Carney of Canada speaking in New York on Thursday.

item.title: YouTuber Defamed Movie Star Using A.I., South Korean Police Say
item.description: They arrested the man, accusing him of fabricating a voice recording to implicate Kim Soo-hyun in dating a minor.
item.dc:creator: Jin Yu Young
item.media:description: Kim Soo-hyun, seen in Seoul last year, had denied the accusations of dating a minor.

item.title: Why Pakistan and Afghanistan Are Still Fighting
item.description: Months after Pakistan declared “open war” on Afghanistan, neither side appears ready to back down, despite China’s efforts to mediate.
item.dc:creator: Elian Peltier and Zia ur-Rehman
item.media:credit: Tomas Munita for The New York Times
item.media:description: An Afghan Border Protection Forces officer, left, and Pakistani Army soldiers, right, at the border crossing between the two countries, in Spin Boldak, Afghanistan, in December.

item.title: Cuba Loses Its Chance at Fuel After Russian Tanker Changes Route
item.description: A Russian tanker seemingly en route to Cuba with a lifeline of fuel has changed direction, a painful development for the island suffering under a U.S. oil blockade.
item.dc:creator: Jack Nicas
item.media:description: Yankiel Perez carried buckets of water on Monday outside a building in Havana.

item.title: Fire at School Dormitory in Kenya Kills at Least 16
item.description: Another 79 students were injured in the blaze, which was the latest in a series of deadly fires at schools in the country in recent years.
item.dc:creator: Brian O. Otieno and Lynsey Chutel
item.media:description: Fire damage on Thursday at Utumishi Girls Academy, a school about 75 miles northwest of Nairobi, Kenya.

item.title: A Quiet Pacific Village Becomes China’s Security Testing Ground
item.description: When a remote Pacific village asked for help with rowdy youth, the Chinese police arrived with a surveillance system. Then came the backlash.
item.dc:creator: David Pierson, Berry Wang and Adam Ferguson
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:description: Fishing on the shoreline in Honiara on the island of Guadalcanal in the Solomon Islands.

item.title: After New Push by the Bolsonaros, U.S. Labels Brazilian Gangs as Terrorist Groups
item.description: Following through on threats, the Trump administration has designated Brazil’s two largest drug gangs as terrorist groups.
item.dc:creator: Ana Ionova
item.media:description: Members of the Red Command being arrested last year in Rio de Janeiro. The U.S. designated the gang a terrorist group on Thursday.

item.title: Italian Police Uncover Dead Mob Boss’s $230 Million Business Empire
item.description: When Matteo Messina Denaro died in 2023, many of his secrets died with him. That began to change with a tip about a wealthy Sicilian woman’s significant assets in a tiny European principality.
item.dc:creator: Ephrat Livni
item.media:description: Matteo Messina Denaro being led from a police station after his arrest in Palermo in 2023.

item.title: A Draft U.S.-Iran Plan Is Said to Be on the Table. Here’s What to Know.
item.description: U.S. and Iranian officials say they are closing in on the terms of a preliminary agreement. Yet sticking points, particularly over the Strait of Hormuz, remain.
item.dc:creator: Erika Solomon and Farnaz Fassihi
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: A billboard in Tehran depicting the closure of the Strait of Hormuz in May.

item.title: Israel to Cut Ties With U.N. Chief Over Its Inclusion on Sexual Violence Blacklist
item.description: The United Nations secretary general, António Guterres, told Israel there had been an “increasing number of cases” of sexual violence against Palestinian detainees, according to a letter shared by Israel’s U.N. ambassador.
item.dc:creator: Ephrat Livni
item.media:description: Israel’s ambassador to the United Nations, Danny Danon, at U.N. headquarters in March.

item.title: In Strait of Hormuz Standoff, U.S. and Iran Deploy Economic Warfare
item.description: The American blockade and Iran’s control of the Strait of Hormuz have created a stalemate that is neither peace nor raging conflict, with both sides attempting economic strangulation on the water.
item.dc:creator: Nicholas Kulish and John Ismay
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Vessels anchored at the Strait of Hormuz on Monday. Control of the waterway is a tool Iran has used as leverage against the United States.

item.title: A.I.’s Big Next Step
item.description: Three major companies are planning to go public with share sales in the coming months. They could be some of the biggest market debuts in history.
item.dc:creator: Katrin Bennhold
item.media:credit: Jason Henry for The New York Times

item.title: The Man Turning the Cockroach Into a Gen-Z Movement in India
item.description: Abhijeet Dipke’s “Cockroach Janta Party” has emerged as the unexpected voice of young people feeling let down by the government and struggling to find jobs.
item.dc:creator: Anupreeta Das
item.media:credit: Jamie Kelter Davis for The New York Times
item.media:description: Abhijeet Dipke at a friend’s home in the United States in May. Mr. Dipke launched the satirical digital group Cockroach Janta Party on May 16, after a controversy surrounding a comment by India’s Chief Justice Surya Kant, who referred to unemployed youth as “parasites” and “cockroaches.”

item.title: Austrian Man Convicted of Planning Attack at Taylor Swift Concert in Vienna
item.description: Prosecutors said the defendant plotted in 2024 to set off a bomb outside the concert, which was later canceled. He and another man were also convicted on separate terrorist charges.
item.dc:creator: Jim Tankersley
item.media:description: The 21-year-old suspect being escorted by guards to court on Thursday. He was accused of planning an attack on a Taylor Swift concert in Vienna.

item.title: Netanyahu Says Israel Will Control 70 Percent of Gaza, Squeezing Hamas
item.description: Israel’s military continues to conduct strikes and seize territory despite a cease-fire with Hamas. Many of Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu’s supporters see the war as unfinished business.
item.dc:creator: David M. Halbfinger and Johnatan Reiss
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Saher Alghorra for The New York Times
item.media:description: Destruction in downtown Gaza City on Thursday.

item.title: The U.S. re-sanctioned a U.N. legal expert critical of Israel.
item.description: Just a few days ago, a U.S. court ordered the Treasury Department to suspend its penalties on Francesca Albanese, whose work on Israel has drawn accusations of antisemitism.
item.dc:creator: Aaron Boxerman
item.media:description: Francesca Albanese, the U.N.’s special rapporteur for the Israeli-occupied West Bank and Gaza, in March.

item.title: U.S. and Iran Move Toward Agreement to Reopen the Strait of Hormuz
item.description: President Trump has yet to sign off on an extension of the cease-fire that would allow the two sides to negotiate nuclear and other issues.
item.dc:creator: Maggie Haberman, Jonathan Swan, Tyler Pager and David E. Sanger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A drone image shows vessels anchored on Monday in the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: Israel Strikes Beirut, Expanding Offensive in Lebanon
item.description: It was the first attack by Israel on the Lebanese capital in weeks and could complicate efforts by the United States and Iran to agree to a peace deal to end the war in Iran.
item.dc:creator: Euan Ward
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Workers at the scene of a strike in Choueifat, Lebanon, south of Beirut, on Thursday.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Maggie Haberman, Jonathan Swan, Tyler Pager, David E. Sanger, Eric Schmitt and Alan Yuhas

item.title: U.S. Strikes Military Sites in Iran for Second Time in 3 Days
item.description: As a potential cease-fire hangs in the balance, an official said the strikes were in self-defense, with targets including four one-way attack drones launched by Iran.
item.dc:creator: Eric Schmitt
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Ships in the water near the Iranian port city of Bandar Abbas earlier this month.

item.title: Cease-Fires, but No Peace
item.description: A truce isn’t necessarily doomed if the missiles are still flying. Here’s why.
item.dc:creator: Katrin Bennhold
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces

item.title: Why Ghana Is Evacuating Hundreds of Its Citizens From South Africa
item.description: Anti-immigrant protests have increased across South Africa, drawing forceful condemnation from several African leaders.
item.dc:creator: John Eligon, Eden Harris and Zimasa Matiwane
item.media:description: A man draped in a Ghanaian flag stands among other Ghanaians being repatriated from South Africa amid a wave ‌of anti-immigrant protests, on Wednesday.

item.title: Trump Threatens Oman Over the Strait, Then Says, ‘They’ll Be Fine.’
item.description: President Trump said there would be serious repercussions if Iran and Oman move forward with a deal to open the Strait of Hormuz.
item.dc:creator: Luke Broadwater
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: President Trump speaking to reporters at the White House on Wednesday.

item.title: Trump Says He Feels No Political Pressure to Make an Iran Deal
item.description: President Trump held out hope for a peace agreement, but said high oil prices would not force his hand.
item.dc:creator: Max Bearak, Erika Solomon, Euan Ward, Luke Broadwater and Michael Levenson
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: President Trump meeting with his cabinet at the White House on Wednesday.

item.title: Canada Rejects U.S. Military Suppliers in Favor of Swedish Aircraft
item.description: Prime Minister Mark Carney chose a Saab surveillance and control plane after vowing to reduce military spending with the United States.
item.dc:creator: Ian Austen
item.media:description: A Saab GlobalEye aircraft in 2019. The airplane is a highly modified version of the Global 6500 executive jet made by Bombardier.

item.title: To Make a Deal With Trump, Iran Wants Billions of Its Money Unfrozen
item.description: The money has become a sticking point in talks, with Iran insisting that meaningful negotiations cannot begin without the funds’ release.
item.dc:creator: Ephrat Livni
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: A crowd carrying a photo of Mojtaba Khamenei, Iran’s supreme leader, during a ceremony in Tehran on Sunday. The Iranian government has been pummeled economically by Western sanctions and recent wars.

item.title: Lebanese Resigned to a Long War, Even if U.S. and Iran Make a Deal
item.description: Few in Lebanon believe that diplomatic efforts to end the wider war will bring peace to their country, as clashes between Israel and Hezbollah intensify in the south.
item.dc:creator: Christina Goldbaum
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A resident assesses the damage in the aftermath of an Israeli airstrike in Tyre, Lebanon, Tuesday.

item.title: What Iranian State Media Says Is in Outline of ‘Unofficial’ Deal With U.S.
item.description: The White House immediately dismissed the report, which detailed terms for reopening the Strait of Hormuz, as a “complete fabrication.”
item.dc:creator: Erika Solomon, Sanam Mahoozi and Leo Sands
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: The Strait of Hormuz continues to be a top negotiating point between the Trump administration and Iranian officials.

item.title: Rubio’s India Visit Yields No Major Deals to Repair U.S.-India Relations
item.description: The secretary of state visited India to reassure the South Asian giant that it can still rely on the United States. India did not gain much from the visit.
item.dc:creator: Anupreeta Das
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Pool photo by Julia Demaree Nikhinson
item.media:description: Secretary of State Marco Rubio and India’s foreign minister, Subrahmanyam Jaishankar, prepare to sign a memorandum of understanding in New Delhi on Tuesday.

item.title: After Decades on the Run, a Leftist Militant Is Sentenced in Germany
item.description: Daniela Klette, 67, was part of the Red Army Faction, which attacked American and capitalist interests until the 1990s. She was convicted of robberies committed later to finance life as a fugitive.
item.dc:creator: Christopher F. Schuetze
item.media:description: Daniela Klette, a member of the Red Army Faction, in court in Verden, Germany, on Wednesday.

item.title: Israel Continues Its Escalation in Lebanon With New Evacuation Warnings
item.description: The Israeli military issued sweeping evacuation warnings for two southern cities, indicating that more airstrikes were imminent.
item.dc:creator: Euan Ward
item.media:description: Villages in southern Lebanon after strikes on Wednesday.

item.title: Could Russia Hit Harder? It at Least Wants Ukraine to Think So.
item.description: Major attacks on Kyiv, followed by warnings of more, come as Moscow is stalled on the battlefield and at the negotiating table.
item.dc:creator: Ivan Nechepurenko and Siobhán O’Grady
item.media:description: A building burning after a Russian strike on Kyiv on Sunday.

item.title: Uganda Closes Border With Congo as Ebola Fears Rise
item.description: Seven confirmed cases of the virus have already been reported in Kampala, the capital, but officials say the country has robust disease surveillance.
item.dc:creator: Musinguzi Blanshe and Matthew Mpoke Bigg
item.media:description: Red Cross workers moving the body of someone who may have died from Ebola, in Kampala, Uganda, on Tuesday.

item.title: Five People Stuck in Flooded Laos Cave Are Found Alive
item.description: The five men, stranded while looking for gold, were found alive after a week in a narrow, muddy chamber. Two people remain missing.
item.dc:creator: Isabella Kwai and Muktita Suhartono
item.media:credit: Norrased Palasing/UGC, via Norrased Palasing, via Reuters

item.title: Why the Ebola and Hantavirus Outbreaks Have Confounded Scientists
item.description: The types of Ebola and hantavirus worrying officials are very different from the species identified decades ago, raising new questions about how to respond.
item.dc:creator: Carl Zimmer

item.title: Global Supply Shortages Deepen as War Drags On, Risking Jobs and Growth
item.description: After three months, the fallout of the closure of the Strait of Hormuz is spreading, with developing countries bearing the brunt of the shortfall.
item.dc:creator: River Akira Davis, Catie Edmondson, Eshe Nelson, Peter S. Goodman and Suhasini Raj
item.category: Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A liquefied natural gas production facility in Qatar before the war with Iran.

item.title: Residents Watch as Israeli War Planes Circle Over Southern Lebanon
item.description: Israel has intensified its deadly military campaign against Hezbollah, the Iran-backed militant group, in recent days, striking targets across Lebanon.
item.dc:creator: Abdi Latif Dahir
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Looking out from a rooftop in Baysarieh as strikes target villages in southern Lebanon on Wednesday.

item.title: After 88 Days of Censored News, TV and Chat, Iranians Are Coming Back Online
item.description: The government is letting people connect with the world after a near-total internet shutdown. But not everyone has access, and those who do wonder how long it will last.
item.dc:creator: Erika Solomon and Sanam Mahoozi
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: At the Grand Bazaar in Tehran this month. For a quarter of a year, most Iranians were only able to access a “domestic internet” of government-approved apps and websites.

item.title: Samsung Unions Approve Pay Deal That Highlights Inequality of A.I. Age
item.description: The agreement all but guarantees hefty bonuses for employees in the top-performing chip unit. Other workers say they feel left out.
item.dc:creator: Max Kim
item.media:description: Choi Seung-ho, right, head of the Samsung Electronics Labor Union, celebrated with a Samsung executive after signing a tentative agreement earlier this month in Suwon, a city south of Seoul.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Eric Schmitt, Max Bearak, Euan Ward, Luke Broadwater and Erika Solomon

item.title: Nigel Farage’s Far-Right Party Is Now Central to British Politics
item.description: Nigel Farage’s anti-immigrant, populist agenda has helped his party, Reform U.K., emerge from the fringe of politics in Britain. But it faces an uphill climb to win power.
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear
item.media:description: Nigel Farage, the leader of the Reform U.K. Party, last month at a campaign rally in Scotland ahead of British council elections.

item.title: Where Time Is Always 15 Minutes Apart From Everywhere Else
item.description: In Nepal, a nation wedged between India and China, a unique time zone is just one expression of a singular national identity.
item.dc:creator: Hannah Beech and Hannah Reyes Morales
item.media:description: Ghanta Ghar, or the “Hour House,” during its renovation in Kathmandu. Nepal keeps its own time zone, 5 hours and 45 minutes ahead of Coordinated Universal Time.

item.title: Inventor of the Basque Cheesecake Plans to Retire. His Secret: He Prefers Chocolate
item.description: Santiago Rivera is widely credited with creating the “burnt” cheesecake in the 1980s, though he doesn’t love the spinoffs it has spawned. Decades later, he’s preparing to hand over his kitchen to his children.
item.dc:creator: Jason Horowitz
item.media:credit: Markel Redondo for The New York Times
item.media:description: Santiago Rivera working the counter at his restaurant, La Viña, in San Sebastián, Spain, this month.

item.title: High-Level British Spy Warns of Expanding Russia Threat
item.description: The director of Britain’s electronic surveillance agency warned that Moscow was getting more brazen, adding that estimates suggested 500,000 Russian soldiers had been killed in the Ukraine war.
item.dc:creator: Adam Goldman
item.media:description: President Vladimir Putin appearing on a screen during the Victory Day celebrations in Moscow earlier this month.

item.title: Iceland Warms to Europe
item.description: The country has always stood apart from the continent. Then President Trump started threatening Greenland.
item.dc:creator: Katrin Bennhold
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Sergey Ponomarev for The New York Times

item.title: Iran Threatens to Renew Strikes Against U.S. Amid Push for Diplomatic Deal
item.description: The warnings came after U.S. forces struck military sites in Iran. Secretary of State Marco Rubio said on Tuesday that negotiations to end the war were continuing.
item.dc:creator: Max Bearak, Erika Solomon, Euan Ward and Michael Levenson
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: A crowd carried photos of Iran’s three supreme leaders during a ceremony in Tehran on Sunday.
